settings.text: Coastline targets the 12 hallmarks of aging and supports the short-term and long-term function of all body systems.
Aging is the progressive decline in cellular and bodily functions over time, increasing vulnerability to disease and death. There are 12 agreed categories, or 'hallmarks' of this process.

settings.heading: Genomic Instability
settings.text: Genomic instability is the accumulation of DNA damage over time due to environmental stress, replication errors, and oxidative stress. This leads to mutations, impaired cell function, and increased risk of age-related diseases like cancer, making it a key driver of aging and cellular decline.

settings.heading: Telomere Attrition
settings.text: Telomeres protect chromosome ends but shorten with each cell division. When they become critically short, cells enter senescence or die, leading to aging and disease. Telomere attrition accelerates cellular dysfunction and is a key driver of age-related decline.

settings.heading: Epigenetic Alterations
settings.text: Epigenetic changes, such as DNA methylation and histone modifications, affect gene expression over time. These alterations disrupt cellular function, reduce regenerative capacity, and contribute to aging by silencing essential genes or activating harmful pathways.

settings.heading: Loss of Proteostasis
settings.text: Proteostasis maintains protein quality through folding, repair, and degradation. With aging, proteins misfold and aggregate, overwhelming cellular repair systems. This leads to neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s, as well as overall cellular dysfunction.

settings.heading: Disabled Macroautophagy
settings.text: Macroautophagy is the process of clearing damaged cellular components. Aging impairs this function, leading to toxic buildup and cellular stress. Restoring autophagy improves cellular resilience, reduces inflammation, and supports longevity by maintaining cellular homeostasis.

settings.heading: Deregulated Nutrient Sensing
settings.text: Aging disrupts metabolic pathways like insulin signaling, mTOR, AMPK, and sirtuins, affecting how cells process nutrients. This imbalance leads to metabolic disorders, reduced energy production, and accelerated aging, making nutrient sensing a key longevity target.

settings.heading: Mitochondrial Dysfunction
settings.text: Mitochondria generate energy for cells, but their efficiency declines with age. Increased oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage impair energy production, accelerating aging and contributing to diseases like neurodegeneration, cardiovascular decline, and metabolic disorders.

settings.heading: Cellular Senescence
settings.text: Aging and stress cause cells to stop dividing but remain metabolically active, releasing inflammatory molecules (SASP). This damages surrounding tissues and contributes to chronic diseases. Removing senescent cells is a promising approach to slowing aging.

settings.heading: Stem Cell Exhaustion
settings.text: Stem cells regenerate tissues, but their function declines with age. This leads to impaired healing, reduced immune response, and organ degeneration. Restoring stem cell activity is crucial for maintaining youthful tissue repair and longevity.

settings.heading: Altered Intercellular Communication
settings.text: Aging disrupts cellular signaling, leading to chronic inflammation, immune dysfunction, and reduced tissue regeneration. Miscommunication between cells accelerates aging, while restoring proper signaling can improve immune function and tissue maintenance.

settings.heading: Chronic Inflammation
settings.text: Aging is linked to persistent low-grade inflammation, known as inflammaging. This contributes to tissue damage, immune decline, and diseases like heart disease and arthritis. Reducing inflammation is a key strategy for extending healthspan.

settings.heading: Dysbiosis
settings.text: The gut microbiome plays a crucial role in health, but aging disrupts its balance. Dysbiosis leads to inflammation, weakened immunity, and metabolic dysfunction. Maintaining a healthy microbiome supports digestion, brain function, and longevity.

settings.heading: What are the twelve hallmarks of aging?
settings.description: The hallmarks of aging are key biological processes that drive this decline, including genomic instability, mitochondrial dysfunction, and cellular senescence. Understanding these hallmarks helps researchers develop interventions to slow or reverse aging. Learn more about each hallmark below.

settings.button_label: Learn even more on the hallmarks of aging

settings.title: Why are these chosen as the hallmarks?
settings.text:
Every hallmark shares these determining charateristics:

  1. Accelerating each speeds aging and shortens lifespan 
  2. Decelerating each slows aging and extends lifespan 
  3. Criteria 1 and 2 have been shown in a range of species from worms to flies to mammals

settings.title: Is the list of hallmarks likely to change?
settings.text: The number of hallmarks was recently revised and will likely change in years to come (we think that biological clock dysfunction should be added, for example!), and each hallmark affects several, if not all other hallmarks. So, think of them as a kind of pro-aging network — disturb one hallmark and it will call on its cronies to respond too. To be comprehensive though, we've ensured that the ingredients in Coastline target each node in the network.

settings.heading: Nervous System
settings.text: Aging slows nerve signaling, reduces neuroplasticity, and increases neurodegenerative risk. Memory, cognition, and reflexes decline due to oxidative stress, inflammation, and loss of protective neurons. Supporting brain function helps maintain focus, coordination, and mental resilience.

settings.heading: Muscular System
settings.text: Muscle mass declines with age (sarcopenia), leading to weakness, slower recovery, and increased injury risk. Reduced protein synthesis and hormonal changes affect strength and endurance. Maintaining muscle health through exercise and nutrition supports mobility, balance, and longevity.

settings.heading: Skeletal System
settings.text: Bone density decreases over time, increasing the risk of fractures and osteoporosis. Joint cartilage deteriorates, leading to stiffness and pain. Supporting bone health with proper nutrition, strength training, and regenerative approaches helps preserve mobility and structural integrity.

settings.heading: Cardiovascular System
settings.text: Aging stiffens arteries, increases blood pressure, and raises the risk of heart disease. Reduced circulation affects oxygen delivery, energy levels, and endurance. Supporting vascular health enhances longevity, heart function, and overall resilience.

settings.heading: Respiratory System
settings.text: Lung capacity declines, reducing oxygen exchange and endurance. Stiffer lung tissue and weakened respiratory muscles make breathing less efficient, increasing disease vulnerability. Supporting lung function preserves stamina, vitality, and immune defense.

settings.heading: Endocrine and Metabolic System
settings.text: Aging disrupts hormone production, affecting metabolism, energy, and body composition. Declining insulin sensitivity, thyroid function, and growth hormone levels contribute to weight gain and fatigue. Balancing hormones optimizes longevity, cellular function, and metabolic efficiency.

settings.heading: Reproductive System
settings.text: In females, menopause leads to estrogen decline, affecting bone health and metabolism. In males, testosterone levels drop, reducing muscle maintenance and energy. Hormonal aging impacts mood, cardiovascular health, and vitality.

settings.heading: Urinary System
settings.text: Kidney function declines with age, affecting waste filtration, hydration balance, and blood pressure regulation. Bladder control may weaken, increasing frequency and urgency. Supporting kidney and urinary health maintains detoxification and fluid balance.

settings.heading: Digestive System
settings.text: Aging disrupts gut bacteria balance (dysbiosis), reducing nutrient absorption and increasing inflammation. Slower digestion and lower enzyme production affect metabolism and immunity. Supporting gut health enhances digestion, immune function, and overall well-being.

settings.heading: Immune System
settings.text: Aging weakens the immune response (immunosenescence), increasing disease risk and slowing recovery. Chronic inflammation (inflammaging) contributes to tissue damage. Supporting immunity strengthens resilience, cellular repair, and long-term health.

settings.heading: Integumentary System (Skin, Hair & Aging)
settings.text: Collagen loss, oxidative stress, and slower cell turnover lead to wrinkles, thinning skin, and hair loss. Reduced hydration weakens the skin barrier. Supporting skin health maintains protection, elasticity, and a youthful appearance.

settings.heading: What are the body systems?
settings.description: The body systems are interconnected groups of organs that work together to perform vital functions. These include systems like the circulatory, digestive, respiratory, nervous, immune, and musculoskeletal systems. Each plays a specific role—from delivering oxygen and nutrients to defending against illness—helping the body stay balanced, healthy, and functioning efficiently.

settings.button_label: Learn even more about the systems of the body

settings.title: Genomic instability
settings.text:
The totality of your DNA makes up your genome, and the stability of this genetic blueprint in cells is frequently compromised. For example, various chemical agents and forms of radiation can directly disrupt DNA, as can errors that take place when your cells divide and fail to faithfully copy your DNA. And it's not just the DNA in the nuclei of your cells that gets broached, for your mitochondrial DNA can be destabilized too. Such genome instability can drive other hallmarks, as in the case of sequences of "ectopic" DNA that have been liberated from their usual borders and end up in the cytosol, triggering an immune response. Another instance is that, following a mutation, the now-misspelled genetic code encodes proteins that don't fold well ("loss of proteostasis" hallmark) and hence no longer function as intended.

Coenzyme Q10

Coenzyme Q10's antioxidant properties help protect DNA from oxidative damage. For example, a study of older adults found that adding 200 mg coenzyme Q10 to a "Mediterranean" diet each day for 4 weeks reduced oxidative stress and DNA damage by increasing activity of the DNA repair machinery. (200 mg is the amount in Coastline, by the way.) Similarly, people afflicted with Down's syndrome have accelerated rates of DNA damage, and coenzyme Q10 supplementation can help reduce this damage in some of these individuals.

Magnesium

Magnesium is involved in stabilizing the structure of DNA and the chromatin it's packed into. Magnesium is also a cofactor for many enzymes involved in DNA processing, including those needed to repair DNA damage. These roles likely underlie why when young adults supplemented magnesium their blood cells were better protected against DNA damage.

settings.title: Telomere attrition
settings.text:
DNA makes up genes, which are housed on chromosomes. The cellular machinery that copies DNA can't get all the way to the end of each strand of DNA, so each time a cell divides it loses a little DNA from the end of each chromosome. Talk about a design flaw. Telomeres are the protective caps at the ends of the chromosomes, so as cells divide during aging, there comes a point at which more rounds of replication would make telomeres so short that the DNA that provides the blueprint for proteins ("coding DNA") would be lost during cell division, resulting in genome instability. To mitigate this, when telomeres become critically short, cells can commit suicide ("apoptosis") or stop dividing and senesce, driving another hallmark of aging.

Lithium

There is compelling evidence from both studies of people who take lithium and studies of other animals that lithium intake can affect telomere length. For example, it is well established that bipolar disorder is associated with accelerated aging, including shorter telomeres. Compared with bipolar patients treated with alternative therapies, lithium users have longer telomeres. The linked study included a series of related experiments, first identifying a set of genes that affect telomere length and then finding that, in roundworms, lithium affects the expression of this set of genes and extends lifespan.

Magnesium

Magnesium has several roles in telomere homeostasis. For example, over 50% of telomeres are located in a scaffold of filaments in the outermost ring of the nucleus (the nuclear laminae). There, some of the filaments that help maintain the organization of the chromatin DNA is packed into are magnesium dependent. Furthermore, experiments on rats have shown that short-term magnesium deficiency reduces activity of telomerase, the enzyme that helps maintain telomere length by adding nucleotides to them.

settings.title: Epigenetic alterations
settings.text:
If your genome (DNA) is the letters that make up the textbook of how to make your body, your epigenome is all the annotations and post-it notes that cover the book, highlighting which sections to pay attention to and which to ignore. Various epigenetic alterations (for example, changes to the shape of the histones that DNA is wrapped around and the methyl groups tagged to some DNA sequences) affect whether genes can be expressed, in turn affecting which proteins get made and so on. The epigenome changes during aging in such predictable ways that various "clocks" intended to estimate biological age are based on patterns of epigenetic tags. Epigenetic alterations with age disrupt patterns of gene expression. As a result, suboptimal gene expression profiles at suboptimal times compromise cellular functions in ways that drive other hallmarks, such as chronic inflammation, loss of proteostasis, and mitochondrial dysfunction. 


Magnesium
There has been clinical research showing that magnesium supplementation affects gene expression, implying effects on the epigenome. For example, a study of overweight adults found that 4 weeks of magnesium supplementation modified the expression of genes that encode proteins involved in metabolic control and inflammation. Regarding the epigenome specifically, effects of magnesium deficiency on the epigenome might even be transmitted across generations. For example, pregnant rats on a magnesium-deficient diet gave birth to offspring with excessive gene methylation, which in many instances reduces gene expression, for the proteins (transcription factors) that would otherwise initiate gene expression cannot reach the section of the DNA that enables gene transcription. As a result of changes to their epigenomes, the offspring had metabolic dysfunction.

Taurine 
In experiments on middle-age mice, adding taurine to their food made the methylation patterns of their brains and muscles more like the patterns of young mice than their middle-aged peers that didn't consume supplemental taurine. (Note that Coastline contains roughly the equivalent human taurine dose to those used in these experiments.)

settings.title: Loss of proteostasis
settings.text:
When it comes to proteins, three-dimensional form is everything. Proteins come in sheets, spirals, and many more shapes, and their conformations determine their functions. Therefore, anything that modifies their dimensions is bound to be bad news. Loss of proteostasis is disruption of protein morphology and comes in many forms, including misfolding of proteins, damage to proteins (for example, by oxidative stress or stray glucose sticking to proteins), and proteins that aggregate together, like the clumps of amyloid that accumulate in the brains of Alzheimer's patients. Loss of protein quality control changes protein functions, which can then wreak havoc on other hallmarks.

Lithium
Some of lithium's neuroprotective effects seem to relate to its ability to increase autophagy and in turn improve proteostasis. Autophagy helps clear dysfunctional proteins from the brain, such as α-synuclein, which misfolds and aggregates in Lewy bodies in Parkinson's disease and some forms of dementia. One of the key mechanisms by which lithium stimulates autophagy and enhances brain proteostasis involves inhibition of the enzyme inositol monophosphatase.

Taurine
Taurine appears to affect proteostasis through actions in the endoplasmic reticulum, a cellular structure involved in the synthesis, folding, and breakdown of proteins. For example, in fruit flies, taurine intake delays intestinal stem cell functional decline by increasing the expression of genes involved in the unfolded protein response, which is needed to maintain appropriate protein folding in the endoplasmic reticulum.

settings.title: Disabled macroautophagy
settings.text:
Your cells each undergo a mind-boggling rate of chemical reactions and activities, and this busy-ness generates damage and waste. To mitigate this, they need waste-disposal systems. Macroautophagy (or just "autophagy") is the process by which cells rid themselves of dysfunctional contents. "Auto" means "self", and "phagy" means "eating", so autophagy is basically cellular cannibalism. This process can target entire organelles too — you might have heard of "mitophagy", which is the disposal of dysfunctional mitochondria. Autophagy is key to other hallmarks, such mitochondrial dysfunction and loss of proteostasis.

Glucosamine sulfate
Glucosamine boosts the production of proteoglycans, glycoproteins, and glycolipids by supporting uridine diphosphate N-acetylglucosamine via the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway. By increasing the flux through this pathway, glucosamine seems to reduce ATP levels in cells, in turn lowering intracellular ATP and hence stimulating autophagy. Interestingly, activation of autophagy appears to be a key mechanism by which glucosamine extends the lifespan of some animals. 

Astaxanthin
In a study of mice that age prematurely, astaxanthin delayed neurodegeneration and cognitive decline and improved motor skills. When the scientists studied the animals' brains, they found the astaxanthin-treated mice had higher expression of the autophagy-related genes that encode beclin-1 and LC3 in the brain. Furthermore, co-treatment with the autophagy inhibitor 3-methyladenine mitigated most of the positive effects of astaxanthin on the brain, supporting the notion that autophagy is central to some of astaxanthin's benefits. Encouragingly, astaxanthin supplementation has also been shown to increase expression of these same autophagy-related genes in people with type-2 diabetes.


Taurine
In experiments on mice, adding taurine to the animals' food increased the abundance of markers of autophagy (LC3A/B) in skeletal muscle, liver, and brown adipose tissue. Importantly, the scaled up taurine dose used in these experiments is about that in Coastline.

settings.title: Deregulated nutrient sensing
settings.text:
Your body needs nutrients to fuel activities required to survive, so over evolution, humans evolved an intricate nutrient sensing system. This comprises anabolic pathways (such as the IGF-1 and mTOR pathways) that support growth, development, and reproduction at relevant life stages during times of plenty. The system also responds to nutritional stress by activating various defensive pathways. For example, the AMP kinase pathway puts the brakes on growth and activates the breakdown of fatty acids and other fuel substrates when cellular energy is compromised. Interestingly, investing resources in rapid growth, development, and reproduction tends to come at the expense of a long life, which is one of the reasons that within species, smaller animals tend to live longer. Dysregulated sensing and responding to the availability of nutrients such as amino acids (as in the case of "anabolic resistance" in skeletal muscles) and glucose (as in the case of insulin resistance) contributes to aging. Deregulated nutrient sensing can compromise other hallmarks, as in the case of changes in the activity of nutrient-sensitive enzymes such as SIRT6 that are involved in repairing damaged DNA.

Magnesium
Magnesium is a key player in how cells respond to insulin, a hormone that helps shuttle nutrients such as glucose into tissues. Malfunctioning of this kind of nutrient disposal contributes to aging and diseases such as diabetes. Insulin sensitivity is affected by how insulin acts at its receptors on cells, and magnesium influences this action by affecting the activity of an enzyme (tyrosine kinase) at a subunit of the insulin receptor. This helps explain how magnesium supplementation enhances insulin sensitivity in people at high risk of diabetes and reduces fasting and average blood glucose in type-2 diabetes. 

Astaxanthin
Astaxanthin affects several aspects of nutrient sensing, including pathways involving AMP kinase and SIRT1. One of the most interesting of these is FOXO3, a gene activated by various stressors, including low energy availability and correspondingly low levels of insulin and IGF-1. (In this way, astaxanthin in some ways mimics Calorie restriction.) The resultant FOXO3 protein is a transcription factor that activates the expression of many genes involved in processes such as DNA repair and autophagy, also inhibiting the pro-growth mTOR pathway. Such responses broadly confer protection against various stressors in ways that counter aging, and astaxanthin's activation of the FOXO3 pathway seems to underlie many of its longevity benefits. For instance, the lifespan-extending effects of astaxanthin weren't evident in worms with a genetic mutation that nullified the function of daf-16, the roundworm equivalent of FOXO3.

settings.title: Mitochondrial dysfunction
settings.text:
Famously, mitochondria are the "powerhouses" of cells, producing most cellular energy. This trite description does them a disservice though, for mitochondria are much more than microscopic energy generators. It's perhaps more accurate to say that your mitochondria are a dynamic family of organelles that motor around your cells to fulfil numerous roles, depending on the context, producing energy and heat, helping to build new structures, maintaining redox balance, and serving signalling roles that contribute to processes such as timely cell death. Your mitochondrial DNA is distinct from your nuclear DNA and accumulates its own DNA mutations over time, contributing to dysfunction. Furthermore, as years go by, mitochondria numbers tend to dwindle, mitochondria don't move around like they once did, and their turnover wanes, contributing to declines in energy production and other hallmarks, such as chronic inflammation.

Ergothioneine

Cells greedily take up ergothioneine via a transporter (OCTN1) that seems to be specific for ergothioneine, pointing to just how much cells like ergothioneine when it's available. Mitochondria are dense with this transporter, and within them ergothioneine helps protect against oxidative stress. Ergothioneine's effects on mitochondria appear to be very relevant to physical activity, for ergothioneine accumulates in muscle mitochondria during exercise training. Ergothioneine then enhances the function of muscle mitochondria: Mitochondrial function partly depends on the activity of the enzyme 3-mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (MPST), and ergothioneine interacts with MPST and stimulates its activity, thereby improving mitochondrial function.

Creatine monohydrate

Creatine has a bunch of positive effects on mitochondrial function. Energy from the degradation of phosphocreatine can be used to resynthesise ATP in cells from ADP and inorganic phosphate. Because creatine supplementation boosts the amount of phosphocreatine in cells, creatine helps buffer ATP levels, mitigating declines at times of high energy demands. In this way, creatine is a temporal energy buffer that can pick up some of the slack when mitochondria aren't able to fulfil energy needs, and by attenuating declines in ATP, creatine also reduces mitochondrial production of free radicals. As well as being a temporal energy buffer, creatine is a spatial energy buffer, for it helps shuttle ATP from mitochondria to sites of need in the cell's cytosol. 

Coenzyme Q10

Coenzyme Q10 gets concentrated in cell membranes and mitochondria, especially in energy-hungry organs such as the heart, brain, liver, kidney, intestines, and skeletal muscle. Within mitochondria, coenzyme Q10 influences energy provision through its redox roles in the electron transport chain. Specifically, it carries electrons from complexes I and II to complex III. Coenzyme Q10 is also used by enzymes that link the electron transport chain to other pathways, such as sulfide metabolism and fatty acid oxidation.

Glucosamine sulfate

The primary reason many people supplement glucosamine is to manage arthritis, a chronic inflammatory joint disease. And there's certainly evidence that glucosamine can help in this context, reducing pain and enhancing physical function. In a longevity context, however, there's more to glucosamine than its effects on joint health. Glucosamine has been found to extend the lifespans of several other organisms, including roundworms and mice. Interestingly, glucosamine's geroprotective effects seem to relate to a temporary increase in mitochondrial reactive oxygen species production, which triggers adaptations that improve cellular functions ("mitohormesis"). This beneficial response to glucosamine depends on a transcription factor that activates the expression of antioxidant genes and hence the production of antioxidant proteins. In the case of glucosamine, the key human transcription factor is NRF2.

settings.title: Cellular senescence
settings.text:
Cellular senescence is when your cells stop dividing, entering a dormant state. It can be triggered by many factors, including when cell telomeres become critically short, when DNA or mitochondria have been damaged, and in response to mechanical trauma (think of the loads on cells in your joints, for instance). In many instances, cellular senescence isn't a bad thing — indeed, inhibiting cell division helps suppress tumors, and cellular senescence is ironically involved in healthy growth and development. However, a large burden of senescent cells can drive dysfunction, due in part to the fact that senescent cells release an arsenal of substances (the "senescence-associated secretory phenotype" (SASP)) that recruits and activates immune cells, amplifying systemic inflammation. The SASP also promotes fibrosis by calling on fibroblast cells and depositing collagen. This is one way senescent cells remodel the extracellular matrix, the scaffold outside cells that affects how cells relate to each other, influencing factors such as the integrity of tissues and ability to heal wounds. It therefore makes sense that many age-related fibrotic diseases are characterized by an abundance of senescent cells in the most-affected tissues.

Taurine
An analysis of the gene expression profiles of mice fed taurine or not showed that many of the taurine-regulated genes are implicated in cellular senescence. In keeping with this, the scientists did an experiment in which they imaged levels of a marker of senescent cells (β-galactosidase) in fat, liver, and brain tissue, which revealed greater levels of senescent cells in these tissues. The taurine dose given to mice in this study is about equivalent to that in Coastline.

settings.title: Stem cell exhaustion
settings.text:
Stem cells are long-lived, undifferentiated cells that can self-renew or be turned into various types of daughter cells. They are therefore key to tissue renewal, whether as part of normal biology or in response to injury. Different organs have their own repair strategies, with tissues that are frequently renewed such as the skin relying on several stem cell niches. Conversely, other organs with lower turnover rates depend less on stem cells. Over time, stem cells can be depleted, and their long lives make them susceptible to damage, hampering their function. The results can be impaired tissue renewal and senescence.

Taurine

Taurine seems to help improve the survival and regenerative capacity of stem cells, maintaining their "stemness" over time. In experiments on mice, adding taurine to their food increased stem cells in the cell populations the scientists tested (gut epithelial cells and hair follicles). Importantly, the dose of taurine given to mice in this study was about the scaled equivalent of the one in Coastline. 

Lithium

Lithium seems to improve multiple aspects of stem cell function, including stem cell multiplication ("proliferation"), stem cell maintenance, and the ability of stem cells to find their target niches (stem cell "homing"). These properties likely contribute to some of the positive effects of lithium on brain function.

settings.title: Altered intercellular communication
settings.text:
To function optimally, your cells must be able to faithfully communicate clear instructions to each other. Cells relay information using numerous signals, and the crosstalk mechanisms between cells comprise both short-range and long-range systems. The former include neural signals, where nerves rapidly communicate with nearby cells using electrochemical messages. Think of this as conversing with the person next to you. Next are paracrine signals, where cells release molecules that relay information to nearby cells. This is more like walking a few houses down the street to chat with people you know. Then there are endocrine signals, where cells release hormones into the blood, with the hormones traveling to cells near and far. This takes longer and is akin to getting in your car and driving down the highway to deliver a message to a friend. Over time, other hallmarks of aging contribute to loss of fidelity in intercellular signalling mechanisms, including neurochemicals such as dopamine, paracrine signals such as cytokines, and endocrine ones such as insulin and sex steroids. This communication breakdown creates a domino effect that then propagates aging to other cells.

Coenzyme Q10
Coenzyme Q10 supplementation can help normalize hormone and cytokine levels in several endocrine and metabolic disorders. For example, in patients with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic disease (what used to be called nonalcoholic fatty liver disease), 100 mg coenzyme Q10 for 12 weeks increased blood levels of the insulin-sensitizing hormone adiponectin, reduced the inflammatory cytokine tumor necrosis factor α, and improved markers of liver health. Similarly, in a study of women with type-2 diabetes, the same dose of coenzyme Q10 for the same duration increased the insulin-sensitizing hormone adiponectin, reduced leptin, which tracks fat mass, and lowered malondialdehyde and 8-isoprostane, markers of lipid oxidation. Regarding the reproductive system, in women with polycystic ovary syndrome, 100 mg coenzyme Q10 per day for 12 weeks helped normalize levels of sex hormones (total testosterone, dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate) and sex hormone binding globulin, which probably contributed to reductions in unwanted body hair growth. 

Glycine
Many effects of glycine supplementation are likely to be mediated by changes in signalling at the receptors glycine acts on. Glycine receptors are among the most abundant in the central nervous system, and they rapidly inhibit the activity of certain cells, thereby influencing body movements, pain, and more. Glycine also acts on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors, and its activity at these receptors seems to be key to glycine's positive effects on sleep.

settings.title: Chronic inflammation
settings.text:
Following an acutely damaging event, whether beneficial (like exercise) or harmful (like a muscle tear), your immune system mounts a protective response to quell the damage. It does this by recruiting blood (including immune cells) to the site of damage to clear and repair the mess. Then, when the repair is complete, the inflammatory response should resolve. This type of acute inflammatory response is beneficial and instrumental to recovery. However, inflammation can go awry, failing to resolve and leading to chronic, low-grade immune activity that consumes resources and ironically perpetuates and spreads damage, earning it the moniker "inflammaging". This has knock-on effects on other hallmarks, as in the case of excessive systemic levels of inflammatory cytokines that impede intercellular communication.  

Taurine
Taurine consistently lowers levels of C-reactive protein, a marker of chronic inflammation, and malondialdehyde, which tracks oxidative damage to polyunsaturated fatty acids in our bodies. Some of taurine’s anti-inflammatory actions are probably mediated by stabilizing levels of endogenous antioxidants such as glutathione and superoxide dismutase in cells.

Inulin
Inulin has consistently been found to reduce C-reactive protein, a marker of systemic inflammation. Inulin probably reduces inflammation in part through its prebiotic effects in the digestive system.

Coenzyme Q10
Like taurine and inulin, coenzyme Q10 supplementation can reduce C-reactive protein. It also keeps other inflammatory markers in check, including interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-α. Coenzyme Q10's anti-inflammatory properties are the product of several mechanisms, including the regeneration of the antioxidant vitamins C and E. 


Glycine
Some clinical research has documented that glycine supplementation reduces markers of chronic inflammation, including interferon-gamma in type-2 diabetes. Glycine's demonstrated positive effects on lifespan in several species appear to relate in part to its anti-inflammatory effects. There are glycine receptors on many cell types, including several types of immune cells and other cells that produce inflammatory substances. Glycine appears to modulate many of these, reducing the production of mediators of inflammation.

Ergothioneine
Ergothioneine is a thiol antioxidant, like glutathione, and its chemistry makes it unusually stable. OCTN1, the transporter for ergothioneine, is especially abundant in tissues that are likely to experience high oxidative stress, such as blood cells and the brain. OCTN seems to localize to mitochondria, so a key role of ergothioneine might be to protect mitochondria from oxidative damage.

Astaxanthin
Astaxanthin is the most stable of all carotenoids and has exceptional antioxidant capacity — over 100 times that of α tocopherol. It therefore protects against oxidative stress in the structures it ends up in, which include defending cell membranes against oxidative damage to their lipids and protecting mitochondrial membranes too. Regarding clinical trials, like taurine, astaxanthin has consistently been found to reduce malondialdehyde levels. Other research on people with overweight or obesity reported that 5 mg per day (about the dose in Coastline) for 3 weeks not only lowered malondialdehyde and another proxy of lipid oxidation (isoprostane), it also increased superoxide dismutase, a key endogenous antioxidant, and total antioxidant capacity.

settings.title: Dysbiosis
settings.text:
Your microbiota is the collection of microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, viruses) that live on and in you, including on your skin, throughout your digestive tract, and within the female reproductive tract. These microorganisms are so abundant that they tend to outnumber your own cells. In good health they live in a symbiotic harmony with your own cells — in the gut, for example, they help digest and absorb nutrients, make nutrients for your body, protect against pathogens, and have surprisingly far-reaching effects on other body systems, including your brain. The composition of the gut microbiota tends to drift with age, and this might have causative roles in aging. As an example, transplanting the gut microbiota from old mice to young mice triggers other hallmarks of aging, such as chronic inflammation, whereas shifting the microbiota from a young mouse to an old one helps reverse age-associated declines in immune function and brain function.


Inulin
Inulin is a water-soluble dietary fiber that microbes in the small intestine have a strong affinity for. The microbes ferment inulin into short-chain fatty acids, and studies have found that, in humans, inulin fuels the growth of “healthy” bacteria, such as Bifidobacterium and Akkermansia. Interestingly, an analysis of semi-supercentenarians (people aged 105 to 109 years) found that their gut microbiota profiles are enriched in Bifidobacterium and Akkermansia.

settings.heading: See below for more Information on each of the 12 Hallmarks Of Aging:

settings.title: Nervous system – This system includes the eyes, brain, spinal cord, and nerves
settings.text:
Lutein and zeaxanthin
Beginning with the eyes, lutein and zeaxanthin are xanthophylls that accumulate in some parts of the eye, particularly the macula in the retina of each eye. There, they absorb short-wavelength light, protecting the eye from the kind of damage that drives age-related macular degeneration. Since lutein and zeaxanthin get concentrated in different parts of the eye (lutein in the peripheral retina, zeaxanthin in the fovea), they’re best taken together. Interestingly, these effects seem to translate to sharper vision in patients with certain eye diseases. Perhaps related to this, these nutraceuticals might also hone eye-hand coordination, going by a study that included astaxanthin as well as lutein and zeaxanthin, as in Coastline. Other research has documented that lutein and zeaxanthin can raise cognitive functions such as attention, working memory, and processing speed. Both of the latter studies used about the same doses as those in Coastline.

Creatine monohydrate
Creatine monohydrate has an impressively large range of positive effects on brain health. Since it acts in part by boosting brain energy stores and countering brain inflammation, supplementation helps maintain function when the brain is under various forms of duress. For example, creatine helps maintain cognitive function during sleep deprivation and hypoxia, and doses as low as 4 or 5 g per day can also counter many symptoms of long COVID and be a helpful adjunct treatment to lift mood in patients with depression.

Lithium
When some people think of lithium, they might think of using very high doses of lithium to stabilise mood in bipolar disorder. And for good reason — lithium very effectively reduces mania in these patients. What’s less appreciated, however, is that trace doses of lithium (which are similar to the levels found in tap water in some parts of the world) seem to be very good for the brain for the rest of us too. Studies of large populations have reported that higher tap water lithium levels are associated with lower rates of suicide. Furthermore, placebo-controlled studies have found that just 400 mcg lithium (the dose in Coastline) improved mood in people with a history of substance abuse, while just 300 mcg each day for 15 months maintained cognitive function in Alzheimer’s disease while patients given placebo deteriorated. Lithium seems to benefit brain health through many mechanisms, but a key one seems to be inhibition of an enzyme named glycogen synthase-3β, in turn countering brain inflammation, improving mitochondrial function and promoting neuroplasticity. 

Ergothioneine
Studies of ergothioneine in non-human animals are remarkably encouraging, and we’re now starting to see its beneficial actions in clinical trials too. For example, a recent series of experiments found that, in mice, ergothioneine helps form new neurons in the hippocampus, a part of the brain involved in learning and memory, through a pathway involving receptor tropomyosin receptor kinase B. The scientists then found that giving adults with mild cognitive impairment 5 mg ergothioneine each day for 12 weeks stimulated this same pathway, and this was associated with positive effects on cognitive function. This is the same dose as in Coastline.

Coenzyme Q10
Coenzyme Q10 is involved in antioxidant defences in the brain and is integral to mitochondrial energy production by helping to transport electrons in the electron transport chain. Interestingly, it’s one of few nutraceuticals that reduces the frequency of migraines. The strength of the evidence is such that the American Academy of Neurology recommends coenzyme Q10 at 1 to 3 mg per kg bodyweight per day to mitigate migraines (level of evidence C) — about what we use in Coastline.

L-theanine
Coastline also targets sleep, in part through L-theanine. L-theanine seems to affect brain function in several ways, including by reducing glutamatergic activity and increasing GABAergic activity, both of which are likely to contribute to L-theanine’s proven anti-anxiety, stress-relieving properties. Regarding sleep, a study had healthy adults take 200 mg L-theanine each evening for 4 weeks. This is the same dose as in Coastline. The L-theanine not only improved sleep quality, but it also enhanced performance in tests of cognitive functions (executive function and verbal fluency), which might be related to more restorative sleep.

settings.title: Muscular system – This system includes the skeletal muscles
settings.text:
Creatine monohydrate
When it comes to supplements to enhance performance in short, maximal-effort physical tasks, creatine monohydrate reigns supreme. By saturating muscle creatine stores, creatine supplementation helps maintain cellular energy levels at times of increased demands, as in high-intensity exercise. This is part of the reason that within a few days of supplementation, creatine can increase power production in activities such as lifting weights, jumping, and sprinting, particularly when these activities are done for multiple bouts with incomplete rest between them. Importantly, creatine also has several actions that enhance recovery between bouts of exercise. This contributes to creatine’s ability to speed muscle mass and strength gains during resistance training, which has repeatedly been found in older adults too.

Taurine 
Taurine has many roles in skeletal muscle. Taurine’s involved in muscle contractile function through roles in the regulation of muscle calcium handling. It also has antioxidant effects in muscle, is involved in cell volume regulation, and taurine supplementation seems to improve muscle mitochondrial function and shift muscle fuel use, reducing reliance on carbohydrate (glycogen) and increasing use of fatty acids. These changes might help explain why taurine supplementation (1 to 6 g per day) can prolong time to exhaustion in endurance exercise in both healthy and diseased people. Interestingly, taurine might be particularly beneficial to endurance performance in the heat, by helping to maintain core temperature.

settings.title: Skeletal system – This system includes the bones, teeth, ligaments, and tendons
settings.text:
Creatine monohydrate
It is speculated that creatine might have direct beneficial effects on bone turnover by increasing activity of bone-building cells (osteoblasts) and/or reducing bone collagen breakdown, but creatine might also indirectly improve bone health, for if creatine increases muscle strength and power, the result will be higher forces transmitted through bones, providing a more potent remodelling stimulus. There’s evidence that taking creatine alongside exercise training can improve how bone responds to exercise. For example, when older men took 0.07 g creatine per kg bodyweight per day (about 5 g per day for a 70-kg person) during a 12-week resistance training programme, they increased their bone mineral density more than their peers.

Glucosamine sulfate
Glucosamine is a precursor for glycosamines. These are concentrated in cartilage, where they're key to strength and mobility. Glucosamine supplementation can thereby reduce pain and enhance physical function in knee osteoarthritis.

settings.title: Cardiovascular system – This system includes the heart and blood vessels
settings.text:
Taurine
Taurine has many roles in cardiovascular health. For example, it affects the function of the cells that line blood vessels (endothelia), helps protect the cardiovascular system from damage through its antioxidant actions, helps reduce cholesterol by binding to bile acids and so supporting cholesterol breakdown, and is involved in the contraction and relaxation of the heart itself. These mechanisms may help explain how taking 1 to 6 g each day consistently reduces both systolic and diastolic blood pressure and how supplementation reduces cholesterol and triglycerides.

Magnesium
Magnesium has many functions in the cardiovascular system, one of which is to promote the relaxation (vasodilatation) of blood vessels by inhibiting the entry of calcium into these cells, which would cause their constriction. Related to this, magnesium supplementation consistently lowers blood pressure in people with poor cardiovascular and metabolic health. We chose a modest dose of magnesium (150 mg) because many of us BRAND NAME lot already consume large amounts of magnesium, so this dose is to bridge the gap between what habitual intakes and what we think is optimal.

L-theanine
Taking 200 mg L-theanine can minimise the blood pressure- and heart rate-amplifying effects of psychological distress. L-theanine seems to have these effects by reducing activation of the “fight or flight” (sympathetic) branch of the autonomic nervous system.

settings.title: Respiratory systemThis system includes the trachea, bronchi, and lungs
settings.text:
Coenzyme Q10 and creatine monohydrate
In a study of patients who have chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, 2 months of 320 mg coenzyme Q10 and 340 mg creatine each day improved various measures of physical performance and lung function. Exactly how coenzyme Q10 affects lung function isn’t clear, but its positive effects on mitochondrial function and inflammation are apt to contribute. 

Creatine used in isolation has also proved beneficial in enhancing respiratory symptoms in long COVID (post-COVID conditions). For example, a preliminary study found that just 4 g creatine per day for 6 months improved numerous long COVID symptoms, including breathing difficulties. Other research by the same scientists explored whether the same dose of creatine for 3 months could amplify the positive effects of breath training designed to strengthen respiratory muscles. They found that creatine charged energy stores in various tissues and boosted exercise tolerance, so it’s plausible that creatine can help alleviate respiratory symptoms in some disease states in part by increasing the strength and energy stores of respiratory muscles.

settings.title: Endocrine and metabolic systemThis system includes the pituitary gland, thyroid, parathyroid glands, adrenals, pancreas, and adipose tissue
settings.text:
Inulin
Inulin tends to reduce blood levels of ghrelin in people who have obesity. Ghrelin is a hormone primarily produced in the stomach that encourages food intake, so this might contribute to how inulin tends to modestly reduce bodyweight. Inulin can cause digestive discomfort when consumed in excessive doses, so studies tend to have people take 3 to 5 g with meals, about that in the AM step of Coastline.

Magnesium
Many studies have shown that magnesium supplementation improves measures of blood sugar control and its determinants. For example, magnesium reduces fasting and average blood sugar in type-2 diabetes, lowers fasting blood sugar and improves sensitivity to the glucose-disposing actions of insulin in gestational diabetes, and improves insulin sensitivity in people at high risk of diabetes too. Magnesium is thought to affect blood sugar through several mechanisms. It seems to influence the activity of enzymes in the liver that regulate the formation of glucose from non-glucose substrates, and it can alter peripheral insulin sensitivity by affecting tyrosine kinase activity at insulin receptors. Magnesium might also affect the production of the transporters (GLUT4) that shuttle glucose into skeletal muscle.

Glycine 
Taking about 5 g glycine before or with a carbohydrate bolus has been shown to reduce blood sugar swings by enhancing the subsequent insulin response. 1. Over time, taking this dose of glycine with meals can meaningfully reduce average blood sugar levels.

settings.title: Reproductive systemIn females, this system includes the ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, and vagina. In males, it includes the testes, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, prostate, penis, reproductive hormones, and sperm
settings.text:
Coenzyme Q10
Regarding female reproductive function, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is one of the most common reproductive disorders. Several studies have found positive effects of coenzyme Q10 supplementation on PCOS. For example, 100 mg coenzyme Q10 per day for 12 weeks improved levels of sex hormones (total testosterone, dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate) and sex hormone binding globulin in women with PCOS.

Regarding male reproductive function, a review compared the effects of various antioxidant nutraceuticals on sperm parameters in sub-fertile men. Coenzyme Q10 had the greatest beneficial effect on sperm numbers of all interventions assessed and the second greatest on sperm motility. Doses used in these studies range from about 100 mg to 400 mg per day. Coenzyme Q10’s roles mitochondrial function make it key to sperm energy production, and its antioxidant properties help protect sperm from DNA damage.

settings.title: Urinary system – This system includes the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra
settings.text:
Coenzyme Q10 
Urolithiasis is one of the most common, painful conditions in the urinary system. It occurs when stones made of calcium oxalate, phosphate, and other substances can form in the kidneys and then move into the urinary tract. In a study of urolithiasis patients undergoing an intervention to break up the stones (extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy), 200 mg coenzyme Q10 per day for 1 week before and 1 week after the procedure improved glomerular filtration rate, an important measure of kidney function, as well as markers of kidney disease such as the albumin/creatinine ratio and β2-microglobulin. 

Water!
Water is technically not an ingredient, and this might seem silly, but one of the reasons we made the AM product a powder is to encourage us Coastline people to drink more water, for water is important to health, and especially that of the urinary system. Fluid intake increases urine flow and dilutes urine. In turn, urine flow reduces the formation of kidney stones and recurrence of urinary tract infections.

settings.title: Digestive system - This system includes the oesophagus, stomach, small Intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum), large Intestine (cecum, colon, rectum, anal canal), liver, and gallbladder
settings.text:
Inulin 
Inulin is a dietary fibre present in thousands of species of plants, including artichokes, leeks, and garlic. Microbes in the small intestine ferment inulin into short-chain fatty acids and inulin doses of 3 to 20 g per day promote the growth of “healthy” bacteria (e.g., Bifidobacterium), improving stool consistency and regularity. There is also evidence that, in patients with type-2 diabetes, inulin supplementation improves liver function, going by changes in blood levels of two enzymes mainly made in the liver (aspartate aminotransferase, alkaline phosphatase).

settings.title: Immune systemThis system includes the lymph nodes, spleen, thymus, and tonsils
settings.text:
Taurine
Taurine has repeatedly been found to reduce C-reactive protein, a marker of chronic inflammation, and malondialdehyde, a marker that reflects oxidative damage to polyunsaturated fatty acids in our bodies. Some of taurine’s roles in immune regulation include helping to stabilise intracellular levels of endogenous antioxidants such as glutathione and superoxide dismutase.

Coenzyme Q10 
Like taurine, coenzyme Q10 supplementation tends to reduce several markers of chronic inflammation, including C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, and TNF-α. Coenzyme Q10 has various antioxidant actions, including helping to regenerate the antioxidant vitamins C and E. Its antioxidant actions might help explain why blood levels tend to decline during times of heightened immune activity, as during infections such as influenza and during chronic disease states. 

Inulin
By promoting the growth of “healthy” bacteria, inulin’s actions in the digestive system likely contribute to how inulin reduces C-reactive protein, a measure of systemic inflammation.

settings.title: Integumentary systemThis system includes the skin, hair, and nails
settings.text:
Lutein

Like other carotenoids such as β-carotene and lycopene, lutein boosts skin carotenoid levels, in turn protecting the skin against damage from excessive oxidative stress resulting from the sun’s UV rays. This has been shown in a study that used the same dose we use in Coastline. 

Astaxanthin

Astaxanthin is another carotenoid that has been shown by several studies to support skin health. For example, supplementing just 4 mg astaxanthin each day for 9 weeks protected people’s skin against photodamage, helped maintain skin hydration during UV radiation exposure, and left users reporting their skin was smoother. This is slightly below the dose in Coastline.
